name: Flat Bench Press
description: The king of chest exercises. Lie flat on a bench, grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width, lower it to mid-chest, then press back up.
formCues: Retract your shoulder blades before unracking
Keep your feet flat on the floor
Lower the bar to mid-chest, not your neck
Drive through your entire upper back

name: Incline Bench Press
description: Set the bench to 30-45 degrees to target the upper chest and front delts. Press the barbell from upper chest height.
formCues: Set bench to 30-45 degrees
Keep wrists stacked over elbows
Control the descent — don't bounce
Squeeze upper chest at the top

name: Decline Bench Press
description: Decline angle targets the lower chest. Set bench to -15 to -30 degrees, secure your feet, and press.
formCues: Hook feet securely before lifting
Keep lower back pressed into bench
Bar should touch lower chest
Maintain control throughout

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
description: Greater range of motion than barbell bench. Each arm works independently, helping fix muscle imbalances.
formCues: Start with dumbbells on thighs to get into position
Lower until elbows are at 90 degrees
Bring dumbbells close together at top
Keep a neutral wrist position

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
description: Incline dumbbell pressing for upper chest development with greater range of motion than the barbell version.
formCues: Set bench to 30 degrees for most upper chest activation
Neutral grip can reduce shoulder stress
Let dumbbells travel slightly forward at bottom
Squeeze hard at top

name: Dumbbell Flyes
description: Isolation movement for the chest. Arms arc wide open then come together at the top, stretching the pec at the bottom.
formCues: Maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Think of hugging a large tree
Don't go too heavy — stretch matters more than weight
Squeeze chest at top, not just hands

name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes
description: Targets the upper chest with a stretching fly motion on an inclined bench.
formCues: Bench at 30-45 degrees
Arms arc wide with a slight elbow bend
Control the stretch at the bottom
Squeeze upper chest at the top

name: Cable Crossovers
description: Constant tension chest isolation using cables set at high position. Great for finishing the chest with a squeeze.
formCues: Set cables at shoulder height or above
Step forward to create tension
Slight forward lean from hips
Cross hands slightly at the bottom for peak contraction

name: Push-Ups
description: Fundamental bodyweight chest exercise. Body forms a straight line from head to heels throughout.
formCues: Keep core tight throughout
Elbows at 45 degrees, not flared
Full range of motion — chest near floor
Don't let hips sag

name: Chest Dips
description: Lean forward while dipping to shift emphasis to the chest. Excellent mass builder for lower chest.
formCues: Lean torso forward 30-45 degrees
Elbows flare slightly outward
Lower until shoulder is below elbow
Control the descent

name: Pec Deck Machine
description: Machine fly variation for chest isolation. Great for beginners learning the chest squeeze sensation.
formCues: Adjust seat so handles are at chest height
Slight bend in elbows throughout
Bring pads together with chest, not arms
Control the return — don't let weights slam

name: Conventional Deadlift
description: The king of all exercises. Lift a loaded barbell from the floor to hip height using a hip hinge pattern.
formCues: Bar over mid-foot, not touching shins
Grip just outside legs
Push the floor away, don't pull the bar
Keep lats engaged — protect your lower back

name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
description: Hinge forward at hips and row the bar to your lower chest. One of the best exercises for total back thickness.
formCues: Hips back, torso 45 degrees or more horizontal
Pull bar to lower chest/upper abdomen
Drive elbows back, not up
Keep lower back neutral throughout

name: Dumbbell Row
description: One-arm dumbbell row with knee and hand supported on bench. Excellent for isolating each side of the back.
formCues: Keep spine neutral and parallel to floor
Pull dumbbell to hip, not armpit
Let the lat do the work, not the bicep
Full stretch at the bottom

name: Pull-Ups
description: Overhand grip chin-up. One of the best indicators of relative upper body strength. Chest to bar is the goal.
formCues: Start from a dead hang with fully extended arms
Initiate by depressing shoulder blades
Drive elbows toward hips
Cross feet behind you to prevent swinging

name: Chin-Ups
description: Underhand grip pull-up that involves the biceps more than pull-ups. Often easier for beginners.
formCues: Underhand grip, shoulder-width apart
Full dead hang at bottom
Chest toward bar at top
Avoid kipping — control the movement

name: Lat Pulldown
description: Cable machine version of pull-ups. Allows adjustable resistance and is ideal for beginners building pulling strength.
formCues: Slight backward lean, maybe 10-15 degrees
Pull bar to upper chest, not behind neck
Drive elbows down toward hips
Controlled return to full stretch

name: Seated Cable Row
description: Seated horizontal row using cable machine. Builds back thickness with constant tension throughout.
formCues: Keep chest up, don't round your back
Pull handle to lower chest/upper abdomen
Squeeze shoulder blades at the end
Controlled forward lean on the return — get that stretch

name: T-Bar Row
description: Row with a T-bar or landmine setup. Allows heavier loading than dumbbell rows with excellent lat engagement.
formCues: Hinge over the bar significantly
Pull handles to lower chest
Keep chest up throughout
Don't bounce the weight — control it

name: Face Pulls
description: Cable pulls toward your face targeting rear delts and upper back. Essential for shoulder health and posture.
formCues: Set cable at face height
Pull toward forehead, elbows high
Externally rotate at the end of the movement
Light weight, high reps — this is for health, not ego

name: Cable Straight-Arm Pulldown
description: Isolates the lat through a straight-arm movement. Excellent for developing the mind-muscle connection with the lat.
formCues: Arms stay straight throughout
Hinge forward slightly at hips
Pull rope down to thighs
Feel the lat stretch at the top

name: Hyperextensions
description: Lower back extension exercise on the hyperextension bench. Builds lower back endurance and strength.
formCues: Hips on the pad, not lower back
Don't hyperextend — stop at neutral
Arms across chest or behind head
Control the descent

name: Inverted Row
description: Horizontal pull using a barbell in a rack or rings. Adjustable difficulty by changing body angle.
formCues: Keep body rigid like a plank
Pull chest to bar, not head
Squeeze shoulder blades at top
Lower the bar to increase difficulty

name: Rack Pulls
description: Partial deadlift from knee height or above. Allows heavier loading to build upper back and lockout strength.
formCues: Set pins at knee height
Same setup as deadlift
Emphasize upper back engagement at lockout
Use straps for grip when going heavy

name: Overhead Press
description: Standing or seated barbell press overhead. The primary strength test for shoulder pressing power.
formCues: Grip just outside shoulder width
Bar rests on front delts before lift
Press the bar over your head in a straight line
Squeeze glutes and abs for full body tension

name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: Seated or standing dumbbell overhead press. Greater range of motion than barbell and helps balance both sides.
formCues: Elbows at 90 degrees at starting position
Press overhead to full lockout
Don't arc the dumbbells inward at top
Keep core braced throughout

name: Lateral Raises
description: Isolation exercise for the side delts. Lifts dumbbells out to the side to shoulder height.
formCues: Slight forward lean (15 degrees) to target mid-delt better
Raise to shoulder height — no higher
Lead with elbows, not wrists
Control the descent — don't let them drop

name: Front Raises
description: Raises arms forward to shoulder height, targeting the front deltoid.
formCues: Arms straight or slight elbow bend
Don't swing the weight up
Raise to shoulder height
Alternate arms or do both simultaneously

name: Reverse Flyes
description: Bent-over dumbbell fly in reverse targeting rear delts and upper back. Critical for balanced shoulder development.
formCues: Hinge forward until torso is near parallel to floor
Arms arc up and out to sides
Lead with elbows, not hands
Don't shrug — keep traps relaxed

name: Cable Lateral Raises
description: Lateral raises with a cable provides constant tension, unlike dumbbells which have a dead zone at the bottom.
formCues: Stand sideways to the cable machine
Pull cable across body to shoulder height
Slight forward lean helps target mid-delt
Control return — no jerking

name: Arnold Press
description: Rotating dumbbell press invented by Arnold Schwarzenegger. Hits all three delt heads through the rotation.
formCues: Start with palms facing you
Rotate outward as you press
Full rotation at top
Reverse motion on the way down

name: Upright Rows
description: Pull a barbell straight up along your torso to chin height. Builds upper traps and side delts.
formCues: Close grip (fist-width) hits traps more
Wider grip hits delts more
Elbows lead the movement
Don't pull higher than your chin

name: Barbell Curl
description: Classic bicep builder using a barbell. Allows heavier loading than dumbbells for overall bicep mass.
formCues: Elbows pinned to sides throughout
Full extension at bottom
Curl to chin level, not shoulders
Don't swing — control the weight

name: Dumbbell Curl
description: Alternating or simultaneous dumbbell curls. Allows supination (rotation) for peak bicep contraction.
formCues: Supinate (rotate palm up) as you curl
Full extension at bottom
Alternate arms or do both together
Keep elbows at sides — don't let them drift forward

name: Hammer Curl
description: Neutral grip curl that targets the brachialis and brachioradialis. Builds overall arm thickness.
formCues: Neutral grip (thumbs up) throughout
Can be done alternating or together
Full extension at bottom
Keep elbows pinned to sides

name: Preacher Curl
description: Curl on a preacher bench that eliminates cheating by anchoring the upper arm. Emphasizes the short head of the bicep.
formCues: Upper arm fully on the pad
Full extension at bottom — feel the stretch
Don't lean forward to cheat reps
Control the negative slowly

name: Concentration Curl
description: Seated single-arm curl with elbow braced on inner thigh. Ultimate isolation for the bicep peak.
formCues: Elbow braced on inner thigh
Curl straight up to shoulder
Full extension at bottom
Supinate wrist for peak contraction

name: EZ Bar Curl
description: Barbell curl using the angled EZ bar. Reduces wrist strain compared to straight barbell curls.
formCues: Use the inner angled grips for more bicep emphasis
Use the outer grips for more brachialis
Full extension at bottom
Keep strict form — no swinging

name: Cable Curl
description: Bicep curl using a cable machine. Provides constant tension throughout the range of motion.
formCues: Stand facing the machine
Full extension at bottom
Curl to chin height
Cable creates constant tension — use it well

name: Reverse Curl
description: Curl with overhand grip to develop the brachioradialis and forearms.
formCues: Overhand (pronated) grip
Keep wrists neutral — don't curl them
Elbows at sides
Lighter weight than regular curls is normal

name: Wrist Curls
description: Seated wrist flexion for forearm development. Wrists hang off the bench and curl upward.
formCues: Forearms rest on thighs
Let bar roll down fingertips at bottom
Curl with wrists only
High reps work best for forearms

name: Tricep Pushdown
description: Cable pushdown targeting the triceps. The most popular isolation exercise for the back of the arm.
formCues: Elbows pinned to sides
Push all the way to full lockout
Don't flare elbows out
Slight forward lean is fine

name: Overhead Tricep Extension
description: Overhead extension stretches and works the long head of the tricep more than pushdowns.
formCues: Keep elbows pointing forward, not flaring
Lower weight behind head in a controlled arc
Full extension at top
Grip one dumbbell with both hands or use two

name: Skull Crushers
description: Lying tricep extension lowering the bar toward your forehead. Excellent for building tricep mass.
formCues: Lower bar to forehead or just behind the head
Keep upper arms perpendicular to floor
EZ bar is easier on wrists than straight bar
Control descent to avoid head injury

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
description: Bench press with narrow grip to shift load to triceps. One of the best mass builders for the triceps.
formCues: Grip shoulder-width or just inside
Elbows track close to sides
Lower to lower chest
Full extension at top

name: Tricep Dips
description: Upright dip keeping body vertical to target triceps. Can be weighted for advanced lifters.
formCues: Keep torso upright for more tricep activation
Don't go below 90 degrees if you feel shoulder strain
Full lockout at top
Feet together, cross ankles behind you

name: Back Squat
description: The fundamental lower body strength exercise. Bar on upper back, squat to parallel or below.
formCues: Brace your core before every rep
Knees track over toes, not caving in
Break at hips and knees simultaneously
Drive through heels to stand up

name: Front Squat
description: Bar on front of shoulders. Requires excellent mobility and keeps the torso more upright than back squat.
formCues: Keep elbows high throughout
More upright torso than back squat
Core braced hard — bar position demands it
Wrist flexibility is critical — work on it

name: Leg Press
description: Machine pressing through legs. Allows heavy loading with spinal support — good for beginners or those with back issues.
formCues: Don't lock out knees completely at top
Lower until thighs are parallel or slightly below
Feet at shoulder width, toes slightly out
Keep lower back pressed to pad throughout

name: Hack Squat
description: Machine squat on an angled sled. Places high demand on the quads through deep knee flexion.
formCues: Feet low on the platform for more quad emphasis
Full depth if mobility allows
Drive through heels
Control descent — no dropping down

name: Leg Extension
description: Knee extension on the machine for quad isolation. Great finisher after squats.
formCues: Seat adjusted so knees align with machine pivot
Full extension at top — hold briefly
Controlled descent
Don't jerk the weight up

name: Leg Curl
description: Lying or seated hamstring curl machine. Best isolation exercise for hamstrings.
formCues: Full stretch at start
Curl all the way to full contraction
Don't let hips lift up (lying version)
Control the return — don't let it slam

name: Romanian Deadlift
description: Hip hinge movement keeping legs relatively straight. Exceptional hamstring stretch and strength development.
formCues: Knees have slight bend — not locked, not squatting
Hinge at hips, push them back
Bar stays close to legs throughout
Feel the hamstring stretch at the bottom

name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift
description: Similar to RDL but legs are kept completely straight. Greater hamstring stretch but demands excellent hip mobility.
formCues: Stand on a platform for greater range
Keep legs completely straight
Hinge until you feel a strong hamstring stretch
Maintain neutral spine throughout

name: Lunges
description: Stepping lunge forward or backward targeting quads and glutes. Excellent for unilateral leg development.
formCues: Step forward until both knees are at 90 degrees
Keep front knee over ankle, not past toes excessively
Torso stays upright
Push off front foot to return

name: Walking Lunges
description: Continuous forward lunges walking across the gym floor. Adds a cardiovascular challenge to the lunge pattern.
formCues: Keep torso upright throughout
Knee tracks over middle toes
Step far enough forward for a 90-degree angle
Use dumbbells at sides or barbell on back

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
description: Rear foot elevated split squat. One of the most demanding unilateral exercises that builds serious quad and glute strength.
formCues: Rear foot elevated on bench, top of foot down
Front foot far enough forward to allow 90-degree knee bend
Torso slightly forward for glute emphasis
Control descent — don't let it drop

name: Goblet Squat
description: Hold a kettlebell or dumbbell at chest height while squatting. Great for learning squat mechanics.
formCues: Hold weight at chest — elbows inside knees at bottom
Use elbows to push knees out at bottom
Heels flat throughout
Excellent for teaching hip mobility in the squat

name: Step-Ups
description: Step onto an elevated platform driving through the front leg. Unilateral exercise that tests balance and hip stability.
formCues: Use a box that puts thigh parallel to floor
Drive through heel of front foot
Don't push off with back foot
Step down controlled — same foot that stepped up

name: Box Jumps
description: Explosive jump onto a raised box. Develops lower body power and fast-twitch muscle fiber recruitment.
formCues: Arm swing helps propel you up
Land softly with bent knees
Step down, don't jump down
Start with a comfortable box height

name: Leg Press Calf Raise
description: Calf raises using the leg press machine. Allows heavy loading with a long range of motion.
formCues: Place balls of feet on bottom of platform
Full stretch at bottom
Full contraction at top
Slow controlled reps — calves respond to time under tension

name: Sissy Squat
description: Advanced quad isolation where you lean back while bending knees, keeping hips forward. Brutal on the quads.
formCues: Hold something for balance
Lean back as you lower
Knees travel forward over toes
Don't sit back — push hips forward

name: Hip Adduction Machine
description: Machine that brings the legs together against resistance to target the inner thigh adductors.
formCues: Set to a comfortable starting width
Controlled movement — no slamming
Full range of motion
Can be done seated upright or leaning back

name: Hip Abduction Machine
description: Machine that pushes legs apart against resistance to target outer hips and glutes.
formCues: Sit upright or lean forward to target glute medius more
Full range of motion
Control both directions
High reps work well for this muscle group

name: Plank
description: Isometric core hold in push-up position on forearms. Foundation of core stability training.
formCues: Forearms on floor, elbows under shoulders
Body forms a straight line
Don't let hips rise or sag
Breathe normally — don't hold breath

name: Side Plank
description: Lateral plank targeting the obliques and hip abductors. Essential for lateral core stability.
formCues: Stack feet or stagger for easier version
Keep hips elevated — don't sag
Body forms a straight diagonal line
Both forearm and lateral foot on floor

name: Crunches
description: Classic ab exercise. Curl torso toward knees using abdominal contraction.
formCues: Don't pull your neck — hands lightly behind head
Focus on contracting abs, not reaching with head
Exhale at the top of the crunch
Don't lower all the way to floor between reps

name: Hanging Leg Raises
description: Hanging from a bar, raise legs to parallel or above. Challenges the entire anterior core.
formCues: Hang from bar with full grip
Don't swing — control the movement
Raise legs to parallel minimum, higher for advanced
Lower slowly — the negative builds strength

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
description: Roll out ab wheel to full extension and return. One of the most difficult core exercises, demands full-body tension.
formCues: Start from kneeling position for beginners
Keep hips low throughout — don't let them pike
Arms stay straight
Don't go further than you can control the return

name: Russian Twists
description: Seated rotation exercise for the obliques. Can be done with or without weight.
formCues: Lean back 45 degrees
Feet on floor or elevated for more difficulty
Rotate from the torso — not just your arms
Touch the floor on each side

name: Cable Woodchops
description: Diagonal pulling motion from high to low (or low to high) mimicking a chopping motion. Excellent rotational core training.
formCues: Rotate from hips — not just arms
Arms stay extended throughout
Both high-to-low and low-to-high variations
Core braced throughout

name: Dead Bugs
description: Lying on your back, extend opposite arm and leg while keeping lower back pressed into floor. Teaches anti-extension core stability.
formCues: Flatten lower back into floor throughout
Move slowly and controlled
Exhale as you extend arm and leg
Don't let lower back arch

name: Mountain Climbers
description: From push-up position, alternate driving knees toward chest rapidly. Combines core stability with cardiovascular demand.
formCues: Start in plank position
Keep hips level — don't raise them
Alternate legs rapidly
Core stays braced throughout

name: Pallof Press
description: Anti-rotation press using a cable. Resist the cable pulling you sideways while pressing arms forward.
formCues: Stand perpendicular to cable machine
Hold cable at chest height
Press forward while resisting rotation
Pause at full extension

name: Hip Thrust
description: Bodyweight or banded hip thrust with upper back on bench. Isolates the glutes through hip extension.
formCues: Upper back on bench at shoulder blade level
Feet hip-width apart
Push hips up until body is a straight line
Squeeze glutes hard at the top

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
description: Hip thrust with barbell loaded across hips. The most effective exercise for glute hypertrophy per research.
formCues: Pad the barbell for hip comfort
Drive through heels
Full hip extension at top
Tuck chin to chest for better glute squeeze

name: Glute Bridge
description: Lying on back, push hips upward. The beginner version of hip thrust, done on the floor.
formCues: Feet hip-width, close to glutes
Push through heels
Squeeze glutes at the top
Keep lower back from hyperextending

name: Cable Kickbacks
description: Standing cable kickback targeting the glutes through hip extension. Provides constant tension throughout the movement.
formCues: Hinge forward 45 degrees
Keep leg straight or slight bend in knee
Extend leg back — feel glute contract
Don't swing your hip — isolate the glute

name: Donkey Kicks
description: On all-fours, kick one leg up toward the ceiling. Targets glutes with minimal equipment needed.
formCues: Start on hands and knees
Keep core braced — don't let back arch
Kick until thigh is parallel to floor
Add ankle weights or bands for more challenge

name: Fire Hydrants
description: On all-fours, open the hip by lifting the knee out to the side. Targets the gluteus medius.
formCues: Start on hands and knees
Keep pelvis neutral — don't tilt
Lift knee to side while keeping hip angle at 90 degrees
Add bands for more resistance

name: Standing Calf Raise
description: Standing calf raise on machine or step. Targets the gastrocnemius through plantarflexion.
formCues: Full stretch at bottom — heel below platform level
Rise all the way to tiptoes
Pause at top for contraction
Calves need high volume — don't rush

name: Seated Calf Raise
description: Seated calf raise targets the soleus (deep calf muscle) more than standing raises due to the bent knee position.
formCues: Knee bent at 90 degrees removes gastrocnemius
Full range of motion
Soleus responds to slow reps
More weight than you might expect — soleus is strong

name: Donkey Calf Raise
description: Bent-over calf raise with weight on the lower back. Stretches the gastroc more than standing raises.
formCues: Hinge forward 90 degrees
Full stretch at bottom
Rise to full tiptoe
The stretch at the bottom is the key benefit

name: Single-Leg Calf Raise
description: One-leg calf raise on a step. Doubles the load compared to two-leg version and improves balance.
formCues: Hold something lightly for balance only
Full range of motion is critical
Don't bounce at the bottom
Own your bodyweight before adding load

name: Clean and Press
description: Olympic lift pulling bar from floor to shoulder, then pressing overhead. Full body power and strength development.
formCues: Clean must be explosive — it's not a deadlift
Catch bar on shoulders in a partial squat
Press from clean position
Master each component separately first

name: Thrusters
description: Front squat immediately followed by a push press. A brutal conditioning movement used in CrossFit.
formCues: Drive from the squat into the press without pausing
Use the leg drive to initiate the press
Bar stays on front shoulders during squat
One fluid movement — squat and press are linked

name: Farmer's Walks
description: Carry heavy dumbbells or implements while walking. Builds grip strength, traps, and full body stability.
formCues: Stand tall — don't lean sideways
Shoulder blades retracted and depressed
Small controlled steps
Grip hard — it's the point of the exercise

name: Kettlebell Swings
description: Hip hinge ballistic movement projecting the kettlebell forward. Develops explosive hip power and conditioning.
formCues: It's a hip hinge, not a squat
Hike the bell back between legs aggressively
Squeeze glutes explosively to project the bell
Arms are just chains — the hips do the work

name: Burpees
description: Full body conditioning exercise — drop to push-up, perform push-up, jump up with hands overhead. Notorious for difficulty.
formCues: Maintain control — not just speed
Push-up should be full range
Explosive jump at the top
Modify by stepping rather than jumping if needed

name: Man Makers
description: Combination movement: push-up, row each arm, then squat clean to press with dumbbells. Full body conditioning.
formCues: Take your time — quality over speed
Maintain plank position during rows
Clean to shoulders before pressing
This is a conditioning finisher, not a strength exercise

name: Behind-Neck Press
description: Overhead press from behind the neck. Hits the mid-delts strongly but requires excellent shoulder mobility and should be approached carefully.
formCues: Only for those with excellent shoulder mobility
Wide grip to reduce shoulder stress
Lower only to ear level — not deeper
If any pain, stop immediately — this exercise has risk

name: Sumo Deadlift
description: Wide stance deadlift variant with feet turned out. More glute and adductor involvement, shorter range of motion.
formCues: Toes pointed out 30-45 degrees
Bar close to body throughout
Push knees out over toes
Hips closer to bar than conventional stance

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
description: Deadlift using a hex/trap bar. More upright torso than conventional, easier on the lower back for beginners.
formCues: Stand in center of trap bar
Grip handles at sides
Chest up, hips back before lifting
Drive through heels and stand up straight

name: Cable Face Pulls
description: Rope attachment cable pull to face height. Essential prehab movement for shoulder health and rear delt development.
formCues: Rope attachment, cable at face height
Elbows finish high and wide
External rotation at end — show your biceps to ceiling
Light weight, high reps for joint health

name: Incline Barbell Row
description: Chest-supported row on an incline bench. Eliminates lower back strain while building upper back thickness.
formCues: Lie chest-down on incline bench
Let arms hang fully at bottom
Pull bar to stomach level
Squeeze shoulder blades at top

name: Wide-Grip Cable Row
description: Seated cable row with a wide bar attachment. Emphasizes upper back and rhomboids more than close grip.
formCues: Overhand grip, wider than shoulder width
Keep torso upright — don't lean back excessively
Pull to lower chest
Feel the squeeze between shoulder blades

name: Meadows Row
description: Single-arm landmine row created by John Meadows. Unique angle that creates an excellent lat stretch and contraction.
formCues: Barbell in a landmine or corner
Staggered stance for stability
Pull to hip — not armpit
Really focus on the lat stretch at the bottom

name: Cable Overhead Tricep Extension
description: Overhead tricep extension using a rope cable. Provides constant tension and excellent long head stretch.
formCues: Face away from cable stack
Rope behind head, elbows forward
Extend arms fully overhead
Keep elbows from flaring — face them forward

name: Reverse-Grip Pulldown
description: Lat pulldown with underhand grip. The bicep involvement allows more weight and a strong lat contraction.
formCues: Underhand grip, shoulder-width
Pull to upper chest
Elbows track by your sides
Good option if regular pulldown causes shoulder discomfort

name: Spider Curls
description: Chest-down on incline bench, curling a barbell or dumbbells. Stretches the long head of the bicep maximally.
formCues: Chest against incline bench (45 degrees)
Arms hang freely at bottom for full stretch
Curl fully — peak contraction
Excellent for bicep peak development

name: Zercher Squat
description: Squat with the bar held in the crook of your elbows. Brutally hard on the upper back and core while hitting the quads.
formCues: Bar in the crook of elbows — use a pad
Elbows stay up throughout
Upright torso forced by bar position
Go deep — the mechanics allow it

name: Glute-Ham Raise
description: Using the GHD machine, lower from an upright position to near parallel using hamstring strength. Incredible hamstring developer.
formCues: Feet secured in machine
Lower slowly — this is hard
Hamstrings do the work, not momentum
Use bands for assistance if needed when starting

name: Barbell Shrugs
description: Elevate shoulders against resistance to directly target the upper trapezius. Builds the traps for a powerful look.
formCues: Arms straight throughout
Raise shoulders toward ears
Hold at top briefly
Don't roll shoulders — straight up and down

name: Dumbbell Shrugs
description: Shoulder shrug using dumbbells at sides. Greater range of motion than barbell shrugs.
formCues: Full stretch at bottom
Shrug straight up to ears
Hold for 1-2 seconds at top
Lower slowly for more trap engagement

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
description: Kneel with ankles secured, lower yourself forward using hamstrings. One of the best exercises for hamstring injury prevention.
formCues: Ankle secured firmly
Lower as slowly as possible — it's very difficult
Catch yourself with hands near the floor
Build up slowly — very high injury risk if done too aggressively

name: Standing Hip Flexor March
description: Standing high-knee march with resistance band. Directly targets the hip flexors often neglected in gym training.
formCues: Band anchored at ankle height behind you
Drive knee up above hip level
Stay tall — don't lean back
Controlled — not a sprint

name: Cable Pull-Through
description: Hip hinge with a cable between your legs. Excellent for teaching hip hinge mechanics and glute-hamstring development.
formCues: Face away from cable machine
Rope between legs at low setting
Hinge at hips — push them back
Drive hips forward to standing — glutes initiate the lockout

name: Dragon Flags
description: Bruce Lee's signature core exercise. Lie on a bench, hold overhead support, lift entire body off bench in a rigid plank.
formCues: Grip bench overhead firmly
Keep entire body rigid as one unit
Lower controlled — this is the hard part
Tuck to regress if needed

name: Jefferson Curl
description: Intentional spinal flexion under light load for posterior chain mobility and strength. Controversial but valued by many athletes.
formCues: Very light weight only
Stand on a platform
Curl spine forward vertebra by vertebra
Reverse back up slowly — this is a mobility exercise

name: Push Press
description: Overhead press using a slight leg drive to initiate momentum. Allows heavier loads than strict press for shoulder development.
formCues: Small dip — just 3-4 inches at the knee
Drive with legs then press with arms
Full lockout overhead
Bar stays over center of foot throughout
